schools.summary: Ambrose: the chief publican's calling proves none need despair, and that not all the rich are covetous (a rich man saved); Cyril: Christ calls the far-off near and gives light to those in darkness — Jericho the scene of grace stooping to the worst.

Gill: Zacchaeus a Jew despite his trade (Jews could be publicans, like Matthew); Cyril: chief publican, abandoned to covetousness (akin to idolatry, Colossians 3:5), classed with harlots — yet counted worthy of mercy; grace choosing the most unlikely, the rich chief of sinners.

themes: grace to the chief of sinners

schools.summary: Barnes: natural curiosity overruled to conversion — God uses every principle (curiosity, hope, fear) to draw men; Titus: the seed of salvation springing up, the obstacles the crowd and 'the multitude of his sins,' and his short stature — earnest desire hindered but not stopped.

Clarke: his short stature compensated by agility and invention — climbing made him conspicuous, even our imperfections subservient to grace; Ellicott: the fig-mulberry (Ficus sycomorus); the wealthy publican running and climbing like a child, earnest desire overcoming every obstacle and dignity.

themes: ran before and climbed a tree

schools.summary: Cyril: Christ saw with the eyes of Deity, naming Zacchaeus though they had never met — omniscience and electing grace; the 'I must abide' a divine necessity of grace; the seeker becomes the sought, Christ's initiative running ahead of and exceeding the man's desire.

themes: he looked up and saw him
I must abide at thy house

schools.summary: Gill: Christ's word reached Zacchaeus's heart, awakening conscience and drawing his soul — the soul sensible of danger flees to Christ with haste and joy; Clarke laments how often Christ's offer to lodge in the heart is refused — Zacchaeus received him gladly, without delay or shame, the fruit of grace.

Barnes: they reckoned the chief publican peculiarly wicked, forgetting he might be a penitent and that the Messiah came to save the lost; the same murmur as Luke 15:2 ('receiveth sinners') — self-righteousness blind to grace, grudging the lost their Saviour.

themes: guest with a man that is a sinner

schools.summary: Lightfoot: he gives half (far beyond the customary fifth) and restores fourfold (the law's penalty for theft, Exodus 22:1); Gill: a public confession in deeds — the covetous extortioner now liberal and making full restitution; true repentance bearing fruit in justice and mercy, answering the murmurers.

themes: the half of my goods to the poor

schools.summary: Gill/Lightfoot: 'son of Abraham' chiefly a son by faith (justified as Abraham was), though also reclaiming his natural descent against those who would unchurch the publican; Christ declares salvation come that day to the despised house — the penitent believer proved a true child of the covenant, vindicating grace.

debates.question: In what sense is Zacchaeus a 'son of Abraham'?

sides.position: A son by faith (chiefly), proved by his repentance
Also by natural descent, against those who would deny the publican is a Jew

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'to seek and to save' — not only to save but to seek (go after) the lost, here the marred publican (vs the child of Matthew 18:11); Robertson: 'the lost' (the collective whole, cf. Luke 15) — the keynote of Christ's mission, justifying his going to Zacchaeus; none too lost to be sought.

themes: the Son of man is come
to seek and to save that which was lost
none too lost to be sought

schools.summary: Eusebius: some expected the kingdom to appear immediately at Jerusalem; Christ corrects this — he must first go to the Father to receive the kingdom; Theophylact: the far country = the ascension, the return = the second coming in glory, with the interval of stewardship between.

Barnes: the historical background — Herodian princes (Archelaus) journeyed to Rome for the emperor's confirmation before reigning; Theophylact: Christ the 'man' (incarnation), 'noble' (dignity), 'not yet King,' going to receive the kingdom (Daniel 7:13-14) and to return in glory — the ascension and second coming.

themes: to receive a kingdom and to return

schools.summary: Gill: the ten servants chiefly the ministers of the gospel, 'ten' a round number; Lightfoot: the same scope as the talents (Matthew 25) yet distinct — each gets one equal pound (the gospel, the same to all, vs the differing talents/capacities); 'occupy till I come' the charge to trade diligently in the interval.

Gill: the citizens the Jewish nation, who hated Christ (traducing his person, doctrine, miracles, persecuting his followers) — 'we will not have him reign' uttered in his rejection and crucifixion; Barnes: the historical embassy against Archelaus; open rebellion against the King, inviting judgment (v.27).

themes: we will not have this man to reign

schools.summary: Gill/Clarke: the return double — Christ's coming in judgment on Jerusalem and his final coming in glory; the king calls the servants to account 'how much every man had gained' — the certain reckoning of stewardship, the day of grace followed by the day of account.

Gill: the first servant like Paul, labouring abundantly; Clarke's key distinction — the equal pound = the gospel (the same to all), the differing returns = differing diligence (not capacities as in the talents); the servant humbly ascribes the gain to 'thy pound,' not himself — diligent stewardship richly fruitful.

debates.question: How does the parable of the pounds differ from the talents?

sides.position: Equal pounds (the gospel, same to all) with differing diligence, vs differing talents (capacities)

schools.summary: Gill: the 'good servant' good by grace and faithful use of gifts; Barnes: the 'ten cities' not literal but figuring great honour, reward proportioned to earthly faithfulness; the disproportion of grace — faithful in a 'very little' (one pound) rewarded with authority over ten cities; Christ crowns small faithfulness with great glory.

Gill: the second servant less useful yet equally diligent — a real but smaller gain (five, not ten); the same humble account ('thy pound hath gained'); Christ owns degrees of fruitfulness among his faithful, rewarding each according to his diligence, the lesser fruit no less truly owned.

Robertson: 'keep on becoming over,' and the parable distinct from the talents (not to be flattened together); the reward exactly proportioned — five cities for five pounds (as ten for ten); Christ's rewards measured to faithfulness and fruit, the diligent rewarded according to their service.

themes: be thou also over five cities

schools.summary: Barnes: the napkin a false virtue — he kept it carefully but never used it, as many keep gifts (learning, property, the gospel) but never employ them for Christ; Ellicott: 'kept all along,' never making any effort; the bare pound returned — the gift received but never traded, the slothful unprofitable servant.

themes: kept laid up in a napkin

schools.summary: Gill: his fear was not the right, reverent kind but ill-grounded; Barnes: he slanders his lord as 'austere' (hard, grasping, profiting by others' toil) — the sinner's false view of God as an unjust taskmaster, by which he excuses his own sloth; a self-justifying, slanderous excuse betraying unbelief.

themes: the sinner's false view of God

schools.summary: Barnes: 'out of thine own mouth' — not granting the slander (God is not austere), but arguing from the servant's own premise: if he truly thought his lord rigid, that should have driven him to diligence; his own professed knowledge condemns his sloth — the sinner judged by his own excuses, left without excuse, knowing God calls to strict account.

Barnes: he could at least have loaned it to the bankers for interest, requiring no labour or risk; Clarke on 'usury' as the money's fair produce; Gill: his professed view of his lord should have driven him to at least this diligence — his utter idleness, when even the least was possible, leaves him inexcusable.

themes: gavest not my money into the bank

schools.summary: Gill: the bystanders perhaps the angels (not to be pressed); the unused pound taken from the slothful and given to the most faithful (the ten) — the gift forfeited by neglect transferred to diligence; the gifts of God entrusted to be used gravitate to those who improve them, lost by those who bury them.

themes: give it to him that hath ten

schools.summary: Barnes: the bystanders' surprise that the pound should go to the one with ten (apparent unfairness); Clarke: the verse omitted in some MSS, perhaps a hearer's remark to correct the distribution; the natural human objection to the kingdom's principle, raised so Christ may answer it in the next verse.

Barnes: the nobleman's principle of reward — the faithful who improve their trust receive more; Clarke: supply 'gained'; from the one who has not gained, even that is taken; the kingdom's law (Matthew 13:12; 25:29) — faithful use increases grace, neglect forfeits it, no injustice but deep equity, no standing still.

themes: unto every one which hath shall be given
from him that hath not, taken away

schools.summary: Gill: the enemies the Jews who rejected their Messiah and his reign, judged in Jerusalem's destruction (and all who reject Christ); Ellicott: peculiar to Luke, the historical Archelaus, spiritually Christ's ultimate victory over the rebellious (1 Corinthians 15:25) — the fearful doom of those who refuse the King's reign, slain before him.

themes: would not that I should reign
the doom of rejecting Christ's reign

schools.summary: Titus: the parable corrected the hope of an immediate kingdom, showing Christ had not yet vanquished death, so he proceeds to his passion; Bede: the parable bore on Jerusalem's doom (the city about to slay him and perish); Christ 'went before' resolutely (cf. Luke 9:51) — the voluntary advance to the cross.

themes: the King going up to die

schools.summary: Robertson on the place-names (Bethphage, Bethany, the Mount of Olives/Olivet); Bede: Bethphage a priests' village; Christ deliberately sends two disciples for the colt — the royal entry planned to fulfil Zechariah 9:9, the King now presenting himself openly to Jerusalem.

Robertson/Plummer: 'whereon no man ever sat' marks a royal progress (like the new tomb, the virgin birth) — the unridden colt fit for the King's sacred use; Christ's exact foreknowledge of the colt and its place a proof of his deity, knowing all at a distance and beforehand.

themes: the unridden colt fit for the King

schools.summary: Gill: Christ omnisciently foreknew the owners' question and prepared the answer — a proof of his deity; Ellicott on the title 'the Lord' — Christ claims a lordship overriding the owner's right (all things at his disposal), the bare word sufficing to release the colt; the Lord of all riding on a borrowed beast.

themes: the Lord hath need of him

schools.summary: Robertson: 'as he had said unto them' — Luke alone notes this exact correspondence; the disciples obeyed without questioning and found all precisely as Christ foretold — confirming his omniscience and deity, and modelling trustful obedience to his word, which never fails of fulfilment.

themes: found even as he had said

schools.summary: Robertson/Ellicott: the same word (kurios) for the 'owners' (lords) of the colt and Christ 'the Lord' — the human lords confronted by the divine Lord who claims it; the question asked exactly as Christ foretold, confirming his foreknowledge; the property of men yielding to the Lord of all.

Gill: the disciples used Christ's exact words and they had the foretold effect — the owners released the colt at once; the bare 'the Lord hath need of him' sufficing, a token of the title's authority and Christ's working in the owners' hearts; all things ready to serve the Lord's need at his word.

themes: the Lord hath need of him
all things serve the Lord's need

schools.summary: Barnes/Ellicott: casting garments on the colt an act of royal homage (cf. Jehu, 2 Kings 9:13); the disciples, lacking royal trappings, use their own cloaks as saddle and honour, setting Jesus thereon as on a throne — humble homage to the King fulfilling Zechariah 9:9 (lowly, riding on a colt).

themes: cast their garments on the colt

schools.summary: Ellicott/Robertson: the imperfect 'they spread' — a continued, repeated casting of garments as Christ rode on, the customary royal homage of carpeting the way; Luke records the garments but not the branches (Matthew/Mark/John) — the multitude owning Christ as King, the acclaim swelling toward the city.

themes: they spread their clothes in the way

schools.summary: Origen: the multitude joined at the descent; Theophylact: 'disciples' = all who followed Christ, not the Twelve only; Bede: they marvelled most at the raising of Lazarus (John 12:18), the chief cause of their praise — the whole multitude praising God aloud for Christ's mighty works, the fitting response to his glory.

themes: praise for the raising of Lazarus

schools.summary: Ellicott: Luke's 'glory in the highest' for Matthew/Mark's 'Hosanna' (the Gentile evangelist), paralleling the angels' Nativity song (Luke 2:14) — 'peace on earth' at his birth, 'peace in heaven' as he goes to win it; Barnes: the acclaim from Psalm 118:26, hailing Christ the King coming in the Lord's name.

themes: peace in heaven and glory in the highest

schools.summary: Ellicott: the Pharisees would grant Christ 'Master' (Teacher/Rabbi) but were scandalized at his being hailed King/Messiah, demanding he check the disciples' 'frenzy'; Gill: they watched to find occasion against him — the self-righteous offence at honour given Christ as King, willing to respect him as a man but not own his crown.

Barnes: proverbial, strong language — the coming of the Messiah is so great an event that the praise is fitting and cannot be suppressed; if the disciples were silenced, creation (the stones) would proclaim him; Christ courageously refuses the Pharisees, vindicating the fitness and irrepressibility of his praise.

themes: if these should hold their peace

schools.summary: Origen: Christ fulfils his own beatitude, weeping as he blessed those that weep; Cyril: the tears a mark of his true humanity and real compassion, willing all to be saved; Gregory: he still weeps through his elect over those who fall away — the Saviour's grief over the doomed, impenitent city, even amid his triumph.

themes: he beheld the city and wept
the Saviour weeps over the lost

schools.summary: Ellicott: the broken, emphatic sentence marks the strongest emotion; Clarke: the wordplay on Jerusalem ('he shall see peace') — she saw not the peace offered; the lament over a despised day of grace, the things of peace now judicially 'hid from her eyes' — grace despised becoming grace withdrawn.

themes: the things which belong unto thy peace

schools.summary: Clarke: literally fulfilled by Titus (AD 70) — Josephus's account of the wall of circumvallation (39 furlongs, built in three days) enclosing the city to famine; Ellicott on the 'trench' as the besiegers' palisade/earthwork; Christ's exact prophecy of the siege, confirmed in every detail — the certainty of his word.

themes: thine enemies shall cast a trench

schools.summary: Lightfoot: against the rabbinic guesses (sabbath, phylacteries, etc.), 'Wisdom saith' Jerusalem fell because she knew not the time of her visitation — the rejection of the Messiah the crowning sin; the total ruin (not one stone left) fulfilled by Rome; the despised day of visitation bringing the heaviest judgment.

Gregory: Christ enters the temple after foretelling the city's doom, showing the destruction sprang chiefly from the priests' guilt; Ambrose: God wills his temple a house of holiness, not traffic, religion a free obedience not merchandise; Christ as Lord cleanses his Father's house (Malachi 3:1), jealous for its holiness.

Barnes/Wesley: Christ cites Isaiah 56:7 ('a house of prayer') and Jeremiah 7:11 ('a den of robbers') — the temple appointed for prayer, profaned into a place of extortion and gain; the worship of God not to be mingled with merchandise, the spirit of the trafficker an abomination in God's house.

themes: my house is the house of prayer
the true purpose of God's house

schools.summary: Bede: the leaders' enmity sprang from his daily teaching, the cleansing of the temple, and the royal acclaim; Cyril: the irony that his words and works proved him worthy of worship, yet they sought to slay him — the hardened malice of the rulers against Christ's gracious, fearless daily teaching.

themes: he taught daily in the temple

schools.summary: Barnes: 'very attentive' = 'hung upon him to hear,' an anxious, cleaving attention (the mark of souls awakened to salvation); the rulers 'could not find what to do,' their malice restrained by the people's devotion — providence holding back Christ's enemies until his appointed hour, his teaching continuing undisturbed.

themes: could not find what to do
the people hung upon him to hear
